---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

.size40[
Exploring real-world, current events cases to illustrate, extend or challenge the arguments made by Vucetic and Persaud (2018)

+ Help us examine the intersection of race and ethnicity with international politics.
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class

1. Review cases submitted to Google doc

2. You brought examples too!

How the coronavirus pandemic is fueling ethnic hatred: The economic crisis is pushing megacities’ dominant groups to be less tolerant and more resentful of outsiders

https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2020/09/18/megacities-pandemics-economic-crisis-is-fueling-ethnic-hatred/

Bhambra, G., Bouka, Y., Persaud, R., Rutazibwa, O., Thakur, V., Bell, D., Smith, K., Haastrup, T. and Adem, S. (2020, Jul 3). Why Is Mainstream International Relations Blind to Racism? Foreign Policy.

https://foreignpolicy.com/2020/07/03/why-is-mainstream-international-relations-ir-blind-to-racism-colonialism/

<br>

Over the last two weeks we've begun exploring critical theories of international relations.

Last week we explored a feminist lens and this week what is sometimes referred to as a postcolonialist or race-based lens.

Today I want to hear about the real-world cases you each brought to class and see how they help us think about the usefulness of this approach to explaining international politics.

<br>

Let's start by going around the room to introduce your cases.

Briefly tell us what you picked and either why it interests you or why you think it speaks to the arguments from Monday.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/14-2-blank-venn-diagram.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Let's compare and contrast the cases

#### - What characterizes our cases?

#### - What do we learn from the similarities?

#### - What do we learn from the differences?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/14-2-research_cartoon.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

### So, if we could go back to the start of the semester would you prefer we had attacked the first 13 weeks of material from a critical ir theory lens? Why or why not?

- e.g. post-colonialist or feminist perspectives?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/14-3-rwanda_photo.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: top center
class: bottom

```{r, out.width='80%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/14-3-Banality_title.png')
```

???

On Friday we'll dig into a research article that brings the idea of race as a social construct to bear.
